policy_statement: Reduce consumption-based emissions by 40% below 1990 levels by 2030 and by 80% below 1990 levels by 2050 (expressed as less than 30 mtCO2e per household by 2030 and less than 10 mtCO2e per household by 2050).
mechanism_description: The city has set targets to reduce consumption-based (outsourced) emissions over time, guiding actions that lower supply-chain and household-associated greenhouse gas emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: Reduce consumption-based emissions by 40% below 1990 levels by 2030 and by 80% below 1990 levels by 2050
primary_causal_pathway: Consumption patterns + procurement set → ↓ demand for high-embodied-carbon goods/services → ↓ supply-chain (Scope 3) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city sets and uses a consumption-based emissions target to steer resident behavior, business practices, and institutional/public procurement toward lower-carbon products, services, diets, materials, and waste outcomes.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced demand for emissions-intensive production and logistics leads suppliers to shift to lower-carbon energy/material inputs and processes (and/or reduces overall production), cutting CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O across upstream manufacturing, transport, and end-of-life treatment.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in global GHG emissions attributable to the city’s consumption footprint, lowering cumulative emissions and climate forcing.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced global lifecycle (Scope 3/consumption-based) GHG emissions from goods and services consumed in the city.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: A consumption-based target primarily functions by driving reductions in lifecycle/supply-chain GHG emissions associated with local consumption, yielding net global emissions reductions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While many actions under the target may improve efficiency (resource efficiency) or reduce waste, those are instrumental levers; the defining intended outcome is GHG reduction (mitigation). It is not primarily about reducing hazard exposure (adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: Some implementations could focus heavily on waste/material efficiency (appearing “resource efficiency”), but the canonical mechanism is explicitly a GHG-reduction target for consumption-based emissions, so mitigation remains dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy framed as a consumption-based/outsourced emissions reduction target has the same primary climate objective—lowering attributable Scope 3 emissions—regardless of which sectors/actions are used to achieve it.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., circular economy, sustainable procurement, behavior change, waste prevention) depend on the actual measures used to meet the target and must be assigned at the individual policy level with supporting evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity arises if a “consumption-based target” is used mainly as an accounting/monitoring exercise without enforceable measures; however, the mechanism as stated implies it guides actions to reduce embodied emissions, keeping the dominant effect mitigation.
key_indicators: “has set two targets”; “40% reduction in consumption-based emissions by 2030”; “80% reduction… by 2050”; “TABLE 5: 2021 CLIMATE ACTION TARGETS”; “less than 30 mtCO2e per household”; “less than 10 mtCO2e per household”
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health benefits via reduced production/transport emissions; potential waste reduction/material efficiency benefits (not specified in the excerpt).
instance_edge_case_notes: Targets are consumption-based and household-normalized, but the text does not specify binding enforcement mechanisms or the specific policy levers (e.g., procurement rules, bans, incentives) that would implement the targets—so classification remains target-setting/enabling rather than regulatory or investment.
